narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on a careful look at your academic history, test scores, background, and other details compared to thousands of past students. You ranked in the lowest decile for your grades in both Year 1 and Year 3 of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance—which were the two strongest factors pushing the model toward seeing you as higher risk for bar failure. Your LSAT score of 34, lower than the average of about 37, came in as the next biggest influence, again nudging the prediction toward failure rather than success. Family income in the middle bracket played a notable role too, contributing similarly to the concern, followed by your racial background as Asian, which stood out against the dataset where most students were White. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was quite close to average and had a smaller but still concerning pull, while being female and planning full-time studies had the least impact of all. In essence, the model ranked your Year 3 decile as the top influence, then Year 1 decile, LSAT, family income, race, GPA, full-time status, and gender—each one tipping the scales slightly toward expecting a bar struggle rather than a pass. Compared to typical students who passed the bar, you fell short on those early grade deciles and LSAT, where stronger showings are common predictors of success. It's a tough spot, but this doesn't define your potential—many factors like demographics and family background are fixed and shouldn't limit you. To boost your bar exam odds moving forward, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher if possible, and build stronger study habits to overcome those early low rankings, perhaps through targeted prep courses or tutoring. With dedicated effort on academics and test prep, you could shift future predictions in your favor and pursue law school elsewhere. Keep pushing—you've got this.
